summary: Gruber speculates about Apple launching a CDMA iPhone 4 on Verizon in January 2011, arguing that CDMA makes sense despite being a dead-end technology because Verizon's LTE network won't have sufficient coverage by then. He contends that the lack of simultaneous voice and data on CDMA is a non-issue for Verizon's existing customer base. He outlines a plausible product timeline: CDMA iPhone 4 in January, followed by a fifth-generation iPhone in June available on both carriers, with the iPhone 4 sliding down to become the $99 base model on both networks. The core argument is that if Apple wants to accelerate iPhone market share against Android, waiting for LTE would sacrifice too much ground.
key_points: Verizon's LTE network will only cover a few dozen cities by January 2011, making CDMA the only practical choice for a coast-to-coast iPhone launch
Simultaneous voice and data is nice-to-have, not must-have — Verizon's existing customer base has already accepted this tradeoff with Android phones
A January CDMA iPhone 4 could serve as the top-tier model for six months, then become the $99 base model when iPhone 5 launches in June on both carriers
Many non-metro areas have Verizon 3G coverage but no AT&T 3G at all, representing a large untapped iPhone market
Apple has historically been conservative about adopting new network technology, as demonstrated by launching the original iPhone on EDGE when 3G was already available
money_quotes: The whole point of expanding to Verizon is to gain market share in the U.S. It's about high-volume iPhone sales, coast to coast.
The message: 'Everyone waiting for a Verizon iPhone: here it is.'
Simultaneous voice and data is nice-to-have, not must-have.
A Verizon iPhone in January would be targeted at a customer base that, by definition, doesn't consider simultaneous voice and data a must-have feature.

If Apple's goal is to accelerate iPhone market share, particularly in competition with Android, then they should finalize a deal with Verizon soon. And if they're going to do it soon, that means CDMA, not LTE.
CDMA is imperfect. Yes, it's a dead-end technology. Yes, it doesn't support simultaneous voice and data.
If the iPhone comes to Verizon, soon enough there will be a model that supports LTE. But Apple isn't going to lead the way on that.
key_insight: Apple's path to competing with Android on market share runs through Verizon's existing CDMA network now, not through waiting for a future LTE network that most customers won't have access to.
